title: The Presidential Suite

jokes.text: They're cuticle shears, John. Do you expect hedge clippers to be drawn from my purse?
jokes.setup: Johnny asks for proper scissors to cut a ceremonial ribbon
jokes.punchline: Moira's dramatically inappropriate tiny cuticle scissors and her haughty defense of carrying them
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira moment - her vocabulary ('drawn from my purse'), the absurd impracticality, and her complete lack of self-awareness about bringing nail scissors to cut a ribbon

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do you have proper scissors? I need to cut this ribbon.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have scissors.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Those are cuticle scissors, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They're multi-purpose. A woman of my stature needs to maintain her cuticles at all times. One never knows when one will be called upon to shake hands with someone important.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, taut? What is that, old English? How bout I hold it tight?
jokes.setup: Johnny uses the word 'taut' to describe how to hold the ribbon
jokes.punchline: Roland's confusion over a basic English word, thinking it's archaic
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Roland's lack of sophistication, though the joke structure is simple

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Taut? Is that like an old-timey word or something?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, Roland, it means tight or tense.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought you were speaking in tongues.

jokes.text: How long is this gonna take, Johnny, should I cancel my New Year's plans?
jokes.setup: The ribbon cutting is taking an absurdly long time
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan suggestion that this simple task might last months
jokes.explanation: Classic Stevie - perfectly dry delivery of an exaggerated timeframe that highlights the absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: At this rate, we'll be cutting this ribbon until next fall.

jokes.text: I think I need to lie down, that blindfold was quite taut.
jokes.setup: Earlier Roland mocked the word 'taut' as old English
jokes.punchline: Moira uses 'taut' immediately after in her dramatic way
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that reinforces both characters - Roland's simplicity vs Moira's vocabulary

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's a perfectly taut observation, Roland.

jokes.text: I want us to look like two very rich people that have just woken up after fainting on a dusty old couch.
jokes.setup: David describing his aesthetic vision for wedding photos
jokes.punchline: The incredibly specific and pretentious image of wealthy people fainting
jokes.explanation: Brilliant David line - captures his pretentious aesthetic perfectly with an absurd but specific image that somehow makes sense for his character

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I want the photographer to capture a very specific aesthetic. Something that evokes the image of wealthy people fainting.

jokes.text: I got thousands downstairs.
jokes.setup: Ray suggesting mouse pads as wedding favors
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that he has thousands of mouse pads in storage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ray moment - his enthusiastic practicality and the absurd scale of his mouse pad inventory

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: I have thousands of them in storage.

jokes.text: No, you get to drive him to the spa where he will indulge in a relaxing five minute scalp massage followed by some light sun.
jokes.setup: Patrick asking if he gets pampered too
jokes.punchline: David's dismissive description of Patrick's role as chauffeur to a minimal spa treatment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's self-centeredness, though the joke structure is straightforward

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you drive me to the spa, and then you wait in the car while I get pampered. So technically, you're getting the breeze through the window. Very refreshing.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, did you say sun?
jokes.setup: David casually mentions 'light sun' as part of spa treatment
jokes.punchline: Patrick's confused reaction to David's euphemism for tanning
jokes.explanation: Sets up the tanning plot well, Patrick's genuine confusion works but it's a simple reaction joke

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a light sun exposure as part of the spa treatment.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Light sun? David, that's just tanning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not tanning, Patrick. It's a controlled light sun experience.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You're describing a tanning bed.

jokes.text: Yeah, see, side by side my Mediterranean complexion makes you look a little anemic so the spray will just even it out.
jokes.setup: David explaining why Patrick needs a spray tan
jokes.punchline: His brutal honesty about Patrick looking anemic next to his 'Mediterranean complexion'
jokes.explanation: Great David moment - his vanity and casual cruelty delivered as helpful advice, plus 'Mediterranean complexion' is perfectly pretentious

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick needs a spray tan because he looks absolutely anemic next to my Mediterranean complexion.

jokes.text: The Under the Sea theme is only available during winter months.
jokes.setup: Ray showing mouse pad samples
jokes.punchline: The arbitrary seasonal restriction on an underwater theme
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Ray logic - treating mouse pad themes like seasonal merchandise with inexplicable restrictions

dialogue.speaker: Ray Butani
dialogue.line: Well, I've been thinking about our mousepad designs, and I wanted to present a summer collection. However, there's an arbitrary seasonal restriction on an underwater theme.

jokes.text: Ew Ted, they made you drink milk on the airplane?
jokes.setup: Ted mentions getting food poisoning from bad milk on his flight
jokes.punchline: Alexis's horrified assumption that airlines force-feed passengers milk
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis moment - her privileged worldview creates an absurd misunderstanding about airline service

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so the airline just gives you bad milk? Like, they force you to drink it?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, no, I chose to drink it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why would you choose to drink airline milk?

jokes.text: Hi, uh sorry there were limited options at the airport gift shop. 'Have a great flight', so sweet.
jokes.setup: Ted apologizes for a generic airport gift
jokes.punchline: Alexis reads the generic message as if it's personally meaningful
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Alexis finding meaning in generic gestures - her optimism creates gentle irony.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm sorry about the gift. I know it's generic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, Ted, listen to this. 'To someone special, may your travels be filled with wonder and joy.' That's... that's about me.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It says that on like a thousand of those.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it's speaking to me. I feel very seen right now.

jokes.text: Yeah, yeah, no need to milk it.
jokes.setup: Conversation about Ted's milk-related food poisoning
jokes.punchline: Ted's unintentional pun about not needing to 'milk' the topic
jokes.explanation: Simple pun that works because it feels unintentional from Ted, though puns are generally low-craft humor

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I think we can just drop it. No need to milk this topic any further.

jokes.text: I'm reminded John of Shanghai and our Party Secretary Suite with its own aquarium, I'd lay in a claw foot tub watching the orcas.
jokes.setup: Johnny talking about boutique hotel authenticity
jokes.punchline: Moira's elaborate fantasy about watching orcas from a bathtub in Shanghai
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira - specific, elaborate, and completely made-up backstory delivered with total conviction

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm imagining myself in a luxurious Shanghai hotel suite, soaking in an enormous marble bathtub overlooking the Huangpu River, and suddenly, a pod of orcas surfaces right before me. I'm there in my silk robe, champagne flute in hand, watching these magnificent creatures breach, and thinking, 'This is authentic travel.'

jokes.text: At Rose Video, he made me purchase the blue-ray of my gritty feminist police drama, 'Miranda Rights'.
jokes.setup: Johnny's business ethics being praised
jokes.punchline: Moira's fictional movie with a legal pun title that she treats as real
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira moment - fake movie title with legal pun, her taking it seriously, plus 'gritty feminist police drama' is perfect pretentious description

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course Johnny has business ethics. He learned from the best. I once produced a film called 'Tort Reform,' and the legal implications were absolutely exquisite.

jokes.text: Miranda is so sassy when she went undercover for that wet T-shirt contest.
jokes.setup: Moira's 'serious' feminist police drama
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's description reveals it's clearly exploitation material, not art
jokes.explanation: Perfect follow-up that deflates Moira's pretensions - Jocelyn innocently reveals the 'feminist' movie was actually trashy

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, there's this scene where the detective has to go undercover at a massage parlor, and she has to take off her top to maintain her cover. And then later, there's another scene where she's in the police station and her shirt gets ripped during a struggle, and you see her bra. And then in the third act, she falls into a river and her clothes get all wet and see-through.

jokes.text: Oh, be careful, John, lest you suffer vertigo from the dizzying heights of your moral ground.
jokes.setup: Johnny taking the moral high ground about business ethics
jokes.punchline: Moira's elaborate sarcastic metaphor about moral superiority causing vertigo
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira - elaborate vocabulary, perfect metaphor structure, devastating sarcasm. The 'dizzying heights' construction is genuinely clever writing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Johnny, standing on that moral high ground must be giving you vertigo, because the view from up there is absolutely dizzying. I'm getting nauseous just watching you perch on that pedestal of righteousness.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm going for an English estate in the offseason.
jokes.setup: Photographer commenting on David's draped furniture backdrop
jokes.punchline: David's pretentious aesthetic description for what looks like covered furniture
jokes.explanation: Perfect David - turning a cheap backdrop into a sophisticated aesthetic choice with specific pretentious language

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: What is all this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a draped fabric installation. It's very chic, very editorial.

jokes.text: Okay, well that's fraud and you're a criminal
jokes.setup: Stevie casually mentions putting her spray tan on David's credit card
jokes.punchline: David's dramatic escalation to calling her a criminal for credit card use
jokes.explanation: Good David moment - his dramatic overreaction to minor transgression, though the joke structure is simple

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You put a spray tan on my credit card?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you understand what you've done? You've committed fraud. You're a criminal.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It was like forty dollars.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Forty dollars?! That's forty dollars more than I expected to see charged to my account for your tanning needs!

jokes.text: You look like a cheese puff.
jokes.setup: Patrick emerges over-tanned from the spray tan session
jokes.punchline: His self-deprecating comparison to a snack food
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective visual payoff - Patrick's self-awareness and the specific food comparison work well

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I look like a Goldfish cracker.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You look like you've been slow-roasted.

jokes.text: I'm practically Sicilian, you don't need that much sun.
jokes.setup: Patrick asking why David told him to get the same treatment
jokes.punchline: David's exaggerated claim about his Mediterranean heritage
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier 'Mediterranean' line, David's exaggeration getting more extreme

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because you need it. I have Mediterranean heritage, Patrick. My skin is a delicate ecosystem.

jokes.text: you look like one of those people who has that skin condition from eating one too many carrots.
jokes.setup: Ray seeing Patrick's orange spray tan
jokes.punchline: His oddly specific medical comparison delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Good Ray moment - his helpful but inappropriate medical knowledge delivered with professional confidence

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: You look like a tangelo with a business degree.

jokes.text: I'd rather not walk in on her and her zumba instructor, don't wanna have to zoom back here.
jokes.setup: Ted explaining why they can't go to his place
jokes.punchline: The implication about his mother plus the 'zoom' pun on running away
jokes.explanation: Decent Ted joke - the sexual implication is awkward, and the pun works though it's a bit forced

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I can't have you over to my place. My mother's been going through some things, and the last thing she needs is unexpected company.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Plus, every time someone visits, she takes it as an opportunity to show them her Zoom meetings from her book club. And let me tell you, those things really help me zoom out of there.

jokes.text: You're starting to sound like me trying to end things with B-Rock on the Backstreet Boys Millennium Tour.
jokes.setup: Ted's serious tone about having an important conversation
jokes.punchline: Alexis comparing it to breaking up with someone from the Backstreet Boys on their tour
jokes.explanation: Peak Alexis - specific celebrity connection, the casual mention of dating a Backstreet Boy, perfect timing to deflate tension

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I have something important I need to talk to you about.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is this like breaking up with someone from the Backstreet Boys while they're on tour?

jokes.text: You know that I can't make big decisions under pressure, that's how I ended up with a pixie cut at prom.
jokes.setup: Alexis feeling pressured to make a big life decision quickly
jokes.punchline: Her comparison to a bad hair decision at prom as evidence of poor pressure choices
jokes.explanation: Good Alexis moment - relatable yet specific example, her self-awareness about decision-making under pressure

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just don't respond well to pressure. Like, remember when I made that decision about my hair for prom? I got it all chopped off and highlighted the same day, and I looked like a angry bird.

jokes.text: Well, I'm sorry I'm not an alchemist, John
jokes.setup: Johnny explaining how to mix hot and cold water
jokes.punchline: Moira treating basic plumbing as medieval science
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira line - perfect vocabulary choice, dramatic overreaction to simple task, shows her complete disconnect from practical life

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is this one of those things where if you touch them both at the same time, you'll be electrocuted?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because I once knew a woman in Marrakech who did that, and she was never quite the same.

jokes.text: it's like trying to force oneself through the eye of a needle.
jokes.setup: Moira trying to move around in the small bathroom
jokes.punchline: Her Biblical metaphor for navigating a standard doorframe
jokes.explanation: Good Moira escalation - dramatic Biblical reference for minor inconvenience, fits her character perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's like parting the Red Sea, except the sea is my hips and the Egyptians are my thighs.

jokes.text: Yes, in the same way road crustations are known to make due in old soda cans.
jokes.setup: Johnny asking if they've managed fine in their current room
jokes.punchline: Moira comparing their living situation to crustaceans in trash, with malapropism 'crustations'
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira moment - the made-up word 'crustations,' vivid degrading comparison, dramatic vocabulary misuse

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Have we managed fine in this room?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Fine? We're living like crustations in a dumpster.

jokes.text: David, those are just ones where you look good.
jokes.setup: David saying the photos they selected are good
jokes.punchline: Patrick's matter-of-fact observation that David only picked flattering ones of himself
jokes.explanation: Good Patrick moment - his gentle calling out of David's vanity delivered with deadpan honesty

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These photos are good.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You only picked flattering ones of yourself.

jokes.text: No, I was being very, very sarcastic.
jokes.setup: David asking if Patrick wants to go back for more spray tanning
jokes.punchline: Patrick having to explicitly explain his sarcasm
jokes.explanation: Decent moment where Patrick has to spell out his sarcasm for oblivious David, though the structure is simple

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you want to go back for more spray tanning?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Oh yes, I would love to go back and have a stranger spray me down with a toxic orange mist. That sounds like a dream.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No. I was being sarcastic.

jokes.text: or I do have head shots from my pageant days that I could mail out to them.
jokes.setup: Discussion of wedding photo problems
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan suggestion to send her old beauty pageant photos to Patrick's relatives
jokes.explanation: Excellent Stevie moment - the absurd suggestion delivered deadpan, plus the unexpected revelation of pageant history

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: If you really want to send Patrick's relatives something, I have some old beauty pageant photos from when I was younger. Those should do the trick.

jokes.text: I think that you are the most beautiful girl this town's ever seen.
jokes.setup: Ted approaching Alexis during her emotional run
jokes.punchline: His earnest, awkward romantic declaration at the worst possible time
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Ted's romantic timing is terrible, creating awkward cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I know this might not be the best time, but I've been wanting to tell you... I think you're really special. I mean, I've always thought you were special, but lately I've been thinking about it more, and I realized that life is short and you never know what's going to happen, so I thought maybe I should just say something before it's too late.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted... I'm literally in the middle of a personal crisis right now.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I know, I know. That's actually why I thought now would be a good time. You know, to remind you that there are good things in life?

jokes.text: 'Cause I did that with Harry Styles in England, it was like too rainy.
jokes.setup: Alexis considering leaving everything to follow Ted to an island
jokes.punchline: Her casual mention of following Harry Styles, dismissed because of weather
jokes.explanation: Peak Alexis - casual celebrity reference, treating major life decisions like weather preferences, perfect character voice

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I could just leave everything and follow Ted to an island.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're not going to follow Ted to an island.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I followed Harry Styles to Coachella.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That was one weekend, and you came back because it was too hot.

jokes.text: I know you, Alexis, and you always make the right decision. Eventually.
jokes.setup: Twyla giving Alexis advice about her difficult decision
jokes.punchline: The gentle undermining qualifier 'eventually'
jokes.explanation: Good Twyla moment - supportive but realistic, the timing of 'eventually' perfectly deflates the compliment

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You know, Alexis, sometimes when you're facing a really difficult decision, it helps to remember that whatever you choose, you'll eventually be okay with it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Eventually?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, yes. Eventually.

jokes.text: Oh, it's my JFK, just playing into the theme.
jokes.setup: Moira asking if Johnny is alright after his presidential impression
jokes.punchline: Johnny's proud explanation of his terrible Kennedy impression
jokes.explanation: Good Johnny moment - his enthusiasm for bad impressions and theme-appropriate behavior

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Are you alright?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm fine. I was just doing my Kennedy impression.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it was devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: Best to leave the voice work to me when setting a mood.
jokes.setup: Johnny's bad JFK impression
jokes.punchline: Moira's professional putdown while claiming superior vocal skills
jokes.explanation: Good Moira moment - her professional authority over vocal performance, delivered with characteristic confidence

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That was not a JFK impression, that was a cry for help. And darling, if you're going to attempt an accent, at least commit to the physicality. I once played a Portuguese widow in a Soho experimental theater piece, and I didn't just change my voice — I changed my entire skeletal structure.

jokes.text: Oh, I guess martinis work better than wax, huh?
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira caught relaxing in the room they said was for business
jokes.punchline: Roland's sarcastic observation about their 'cleaning' supplies
jokes.explanation: Good Roland moment - catching Johnny in hypocrisy with simple but effective sarcasm

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I see you two are really putting those cleaning supplies to good use.

jokes.text: we'd be swapping mattresses.
jokes.setup: Roland suggesting they swap houses for the weekend
jokes.punchline: His innocent clarification that makes it more awkward
jokes.explanation: Decent Roland moment - his attempt to clarify makes the suggestion more inappropriate

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We could swap houses for the weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, you stay at my place, I stay at yours. With our families.

jokes.text: The man's from Boston. - John! - Coming Moira And FDR said that.
jokes.setup: Argument about JFK impressions and presidential quotes
jokes.punchline: The overlapping corrections and Johnny's continued wrong information
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Johnny's bad impression subplot, though the overlapping dialogue makes it less sharp

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ask not what your country can do for you, ask what you can do for your country. That's JFK.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's not even close to what he said.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's exactly what he said. I do a very accurate JFK impression.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your JFK sounds like a man having a stroke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, he did have back problems, so maybe that's historically accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's not how any of this works.

jokes.text: More like I politely but forcefully asked everyone to leave.
jokes.setup: Ted asking if Alexis closed down the whole café
jokes.punchline: Her polite euphemism for what was probably aggressive behavior
jokes.explanation: Good Alexis moment - her attempt to make forceful behavior sound sophisticated and polite

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was very firm with them.

jokes.text: Also, I may have forgotten about the whole milk situation.
jokes.setup: Alexis serving dairy-heavy comfort food to Ted
jokes.punchline: Her realization about his milk-related food poisoning
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Alexis's well-meaning but thoughtless gesture, creates nice irony

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, you're not looking good. Here, I made you some mac and cheese, and a nice creamy soup, and some ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I think I'm lactose intolerant.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, why didn't you say something?!

jokes.text: I skipped the class that taught us how to... care for large mammals and I don't know how to get it to leave.
jokes.setup: Alexis saying there's an elephant in the room
jokes.punchline: Her literal interpretation and admission of not knowing elephant care
jokes.explanation: Great Alexis moment - taking the metaphor literally while still understanding the concept, perfect character voice

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: There's an elephant in the room.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, there's literally an elephant in the room. I don't know how it got here, and I certainly don't know how to take care of it.

jokes.text: Actually, those flies are an invaluable piece of the puzzle in the study of evolution, but... yes we do have our fingers crossed that there are still a few more out there.
jokes.setup: Alexis dismissing his research flies as 'gross little flies'
jokes.punchline: Ted's earnest scientific correction followed by hopeful enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Good Ted moment - his inability to not correct scientific misconceptions even during breakup, shows his character priorities

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Actually, they're not gross little flies. They're fruit flies, and they're a cornerstone of modern genetics research. Decades of scientific advancement wouldn't be possible without them.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So... does that mean you'd like to help me with my research?

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira spend a night at the presidential suite in the new motel, while Alexis receives a surprise visitor.
insight: Wordplay and absurdist humor sustain a quieter finale despite below-average impact scores.
